signals.title: USD/JPY — Geopolitical Shock + Carry + Options
signals.text: USD/JPY at 158.91 — Markets pricing a 'peace pivot' as Iran deal hopes crash Oil to $95, driving a bearish gap and USD weakness. Despite the headline-driven retreat, the 278bp Fed-BoJ OIS carry advantage (SOFR 3.51% vs TONA 0.73%) remains the structural anchor. JPY COT LF net short -81,624 (31.2% long) confirms a crowded carry trade, while the 25d RR at -1.41 signals the options market is paying a moderate premium for JPY protection (downside USD/JPY) amid intervention fears. Carry dominates the long-term regime, but geopolitical de-escalation triggers immediate JPY short-covering.

signals.title: NZD/USD — COT vs OIS Divergence + RBNZ
signals.text: NZD/USD at 0.5872 — COT LF net short -15,435 (28.0% long) shows speculators fading the RBNZ despite a hawkish OIS bias (Hike bias priced for 27 May). With NZD real rates at -0.83% (most accommodative in G8), the currency faces structural pressure, yet a hawkish hold or surprise hike on 27 May introduces significant short-squeeze risk against the crowded spec short. NZD/USD vs NZX 50 correlation at +0.386 (z=+2.03σ) indicates a break from the -0.232 norm, suggesting NZD is currently trading as a high-beta risk proxy rather than on domestic equity fundamentals.

signals.title: AUD/USD — Volatility + Positioning + Retail Divergence
signals.text: AUD/USD at 0.7173 — IV at 13.8% vs HV30 at 8.9% generates a +4.9% Vol Risk Premium, signaling aggressive institutional hedging of the upside. This aligns with COT LF net long +57,180 (74.5% long), showing heavy institutional backing. Conversely, retail sentiment is 75% short, creating a classic institutional vs. retail divergence. The 84bp AUD carry premium (AONIA 4.35% vs SOFR 3.51%) supports the long bias, but the extreme VRP suggests the market is paying up for protection against a trend exhaustion. Watch for retail capitulation if price tests 0.7250.

signals.title: EUR/USD — Volatility Premium + CESI Alignment
signals.text: EUR/USD at 1.1641 — IV at 7.1% vs HV30 at 4.9% (VRP +2.2%) indicates elevated hedging demand despite a relatively stable spot price. The positive USD CESI (net +24) supports the 175bp Fed-ECB carry advantage (Fed 3.75% vs ECB 2.00%), keeping EUR upside structurally limited. While the 25d RR at -0.35 shows only a mild put skew, the combination of a restrictive US real rate (+1.09%) vs an accommodative EUR real rate (-0.32%) favors USD on any data-driven rallies. Watch the ECB meeting on 11 June for a shift in the 'Hold' bias.
